skill.name: writing-great-skills
skill.description: Reference for writing and editing skills well — the vocabulary and principles that make a skill predictable.
skill.summary: A documentation skill that defines the vocabulary and principles for writing predictable, well-structured Claude skills.
skill.author: mattpocock

security_audit.summary: The skill is pure markdown documentation containing a glossary and principles for writing Claude skills. Static analysis flagged 57 potential issues, but all are false positives: 'backtick execution' patterns are markdown inline code formatting (e.g., SKILL.md, GLOSSARY.md), and 'weak cryptographic algorithm' matches are words in prose text. No executable code, scripts, network access, or file system operations exist. Safe to publish.

low_findings.title: Markdown backticks flagged as shell execution
low_findings.description: Static analysis flagged backtick characters as 'Ruby/shell backtick execution'. These are markdown inline code formatting (e.g., SKILL.md, GLOSSARY.md) in documentation prose, not code execution patterns.

content.value_statement: Writing effective Claude skills is hard without a shared vocabulary. This skill provides the terminology and principles that make skill authoring systematic and repeatable.
actual_capabilities: Defines core vocabulary for skill design (predictability, granularity, context load)
Explains model-invoked versus user-invoked skills and their trade-offs
Describes progressive disclosure and the information hierarchy
Lists common failure modes (premature completion, duplication, sprawl, no-ops)
Provides guidance on writing effective skill descriptions
Documents the concept of leading words and their role in predictability
limitations: Does not generate skills automatically — it is a reference guide
Does not validate existing skills for quality
Assumes the reader is already creating skills for Claude
User-invoked only — not auto-triggered by the agent

use_cases.title: New skill authors learning the basics
use_cases.description: A developer new to Claude skills reads this to understand core concepts before writing their first skill.

use_cases.title: Experienced authors refining their skills
use_cases.description: An author of multiple skills consults the glossary to diagnose issues like premature completion or sprawl.

use_cases.title: Team leads establishing authoring standards
use_cases.description: A team lead uses the vocabulary and principles to set quality standards for skills in their organization.

prompt_templates.title: Learn the basic vocabulary
prompt_templates.prompt: Explain the key terms defined in this skill's glossary and how they relate to each other.

prompt_templates.title: Choose invocation type
prompt_templates.prompt: Help me decide whether my new skill should be model-invoked or user-invoked based on its purpose.

prompt_templates.title: Diagnose a skill problem
prompt_templates.prompt: My skill keeps ending steps too early. Use the failure modes in this glossary to diagnose and fix it.

prompt_templates.title: Refactor for progressive disclosure
prompt_templates.prompt: My skill's SKILL.md is too long. Apply progressive disclosure principles to restructure it.

output_examples.input: Should I make a skill model-invoked or user-invoked?
output: Model-invoked: keep the description so the agent can fire it autonomously
User-invoked: strip the description so only humans can reach it
Model-invocation adds context load on every turn
User-invocation adds cognitive load on the human
Ending a step before it is genuinely done
Caused by vague completion criteria or visible post-completion steps
Defence: sharpen the criterion first, hide later steps only if needed

best_practices: Front-load the leading word in model-invoked skill descriptions
Write checkable and exhaustive completion criteria for every step
Use progressive disclosure to push reference behind context pointers
anti_patterns: Writing vague completion criteria like 'understanding reached'
Repeating the same meaning in multiple sections of a skill
Accumulating stale content without a pruning discipline

faq.question: Is this skill auto-invoked by the agent?
faq.answer: No. It is user-invoked only — you must type its name to trigger it.

faq.question: Can this skill help me write a new skill?
faq.answer: Yes. It provides the vocabulary and principles to guide your skill design decisions.

faq.question: What is a leading word?
faq.answer: A compact concept from the model's pretraining that anchors behavior in few tokens, like 'lesson' or 'tracer bullets'.

faq.question: How do I know if my skill has too much content?
faq.answer: If the SKILL.md is long enough to hurt readability, consider progressive disclosure to push reference into linked files.

faq.question: What causes premature completion?
faq.answer: Vague completion criteria combined with visible post-completion steps create a pull forward that ends steps early.

faq.question: Does this skill generate skills automatically?
faq.answer: No. It is a reference guide for human authors — the agent does not use it to write skills on your behalf.
